column1: # Terms are pinned to the two LO parts:
#   1. Discuss ethical issues raised by emerging healthcare technology
#   2. Apply ethical principles to evaluate them (the four principles)
# Technical deep-dives (how a neural net works

column2: The ethical principle that a patient has the right to make informed decisions about their own body and care.
column4: Autonomy is threatened when an AI decides a treatment plan without the patient understanding why.

column2: The ethical principle of acting for the patient's benefit.
column4: A telehealth program that reaches rural patients who had no nearby clinic serves beneficence.

column2: The ethical principle of "first, do no harm" — avoiding actions that injure the patient.
column4: A wearable that raises false alarms night after night violates non-maleficence by causing anxiety.

column2: The ethical principle of fair distribution of benefits, risks, and access.
column4: Justice asks who can afford the new technology and who is left out.

column2: A pattern in which an AI system performs worse for some groups because its training data under-represented them or encoded historical inequities.
column4: Algorithmic bias in a skin-cancer app can mean lower accuracy for darker skin tones.

column2: The process of explaining a procedure, test, or data use to a patient in terms they understand, so they can agree or refuse.
column4: Informed consent for a genetic test covers what is tested, who sees the result, and what it may reveal about relatives.

column2: Treating someone less favorably — in hiring, insurance, or other decisions — because of their genetic information.
column4: GINA blocks genetic discrimination in health insurance and employment, but not in life or long-term-care insurance.

column2: The delivery of clinical care by phone or video rather than in person.
column4: Telehealth expanded access during the pandemic but exposed a digital divide for patients without broadband.

column2: The gap between people who have reliable internet, devices, and digital skills and people who do not.
column4: The digital divide turns a telehealth benefit into an access inequity for rural and low-income patients.

column2: A consumer health device, like a smartwatch or ring, that continuously collects body data such as heart rate or sleep.
column4: A wearable that shares step counts with an employer wellness program raises privacy questions.

column2: A written document in which a person states the medical care they want — or do not want — if they cannot speak for themselves.
column4: An advance directive can say a patient does not want to be kept alive by a ventilator past a certain point.

column2: Medical technology — such as ventilators, ECMO, or artificial nutrition — that keeps a patient alive when their body cannot do so on its own.
column4: Life-sustaining technology can extend life, but it can also extend dying past what the patient would have wanted.

column2: Clear assignment of who is responsible when a decision — human or machine-made — produces harm.
column4: Accountability for an AI diagnostic error is contested: the developer? the hospital? the clinician who trusted it?
